rubric_identity.one_sentence_purpose: Evaluate candidate CVs for architecture-heavy, production-focused AI platform roles where senior IC or player-coach scope dominates and delivery, reliability, evaluation, and stakeholder translation matter more than formal org leadership.
rubric_identity.core_persona: Architect-first player-coach who turns AI concepts into production architecture, integration patterns, and reliable delivery while mentoring and translating across teams without overstating org ownership.
job_overlay_notes: Despite the 'Head of AI' label, this market behaves like Principal/Lead AI Architect roles with senior IC scope in 86.8% of positions.
Platform design is near-universal at 94.7%, while hiring is only 5.3% and budget/P&L is 0%.
Adjust for company-specific stack emphasis but do not overfit to any single technology since no hard skill exceeds 36.8% prevalence.
Remote-first positions represent 50% of the market, so distributed delivery credibility matters.
Research framing is a minority signal at 10.5%; PhD requirement is 0%.

what_good_looks_like: Strongest 4-6 keywords appear 2-3 times across headline, profile, experience, and skills sections.
Architecture-forward terminology: platform architecture, system design, enterprise architecture, scalability, microservices.
Production AI quality terms: evaluation, reliability, observability, guardrails, governance.
Applied AI integration terms: RAG, workflow orchestration, agents, retrieval when true to experience.
Soft-skill keywords aligned to category: collaboration, communication, stakeholder alignment, mentoring.
common_failures: Keyword stuffing with technologies not evidenced in the candidate record.
Missing architecture and platform design terminology despite being 94.7% prevalence signals.
Over-indexing on a single stack when no technology exceeds 36.8% category prevalence.
Using executive AI titles when the role scope is senior IC or player-coach.
Omitting production AI quality terms like evaluation, reliability, and guardrails.

what_to_adjust_per_jd: Match cloud provider terminology to JD requirements: AWS 23.7%, Azure 10.5%, multi-cloud 23.7%.
Adjust container and orchestration terms based on JD: Kubernetes 23.7%, Docker 21.1%.
Surface domain-specific compliance terms only when JD explicitly requires them.
Mirror JD language on AI methods like RAG, agents, or evaluation frameworks when evidenced.
company_domain_overrides: Consulting roles (13.2%): emphasize stakeholder translation, partner API integration, enterprise systems.
Enterprise SaaS (10.5%): emphasize scalability, multi-tenant architecture, integration patterns.
Cybersecurity (7.9%): surface guardrails, policy controls, and auditability when evidenced.
Fintech (7.9%): include reliability, observability, and any relevant compliance evidence.
region_overrides: EEA remote-first positions (50%): include distributed delivery and cross-timezone collaboration signals.
Do not claim broad EEA governance authority without explicit documented ownership.
GDPR and data privacy terms appear only 5.3% each; use only when directly evidenced.

9_10: All category table-stakes keywords present with appropriate frequency: architecture, platform design, reliability, collaboration.
Differentiator keywords strategically placed: evaluation, guardrails, mentoring, delivery.
Stack terms precisely match evidenced experience and JD requirements.
No keyword inflation or unsupported technology claims.
Headline and profile are architecture-forward with production AI platform emphasis.
7_8: Most category keywords present but frequency or placement could be optimized.
Architecture and delivery terminology is strong but AI quality terms are underweighted.
Stack alignment is good but misses 1-2 JD-specific technologies that are evidenced.
Minor gaps in soft-skill keyword coverage.
5_6: Core architecture keywords present but production AI quality terms are sparse.
Keyword placement is inconsistent across sections.
Some stack terms appear without clear evidence support.
Missing collaboration or stakeholder alignment terminology.
0_4: Missing fundamental category keywords like architecture, platform design, or delivery.
Significant keyword stuffing with unevidenced technologies.
Executive AI titles used without supporting scope evidence.
No production AI quality terminology despite being category differentiators.

red_flags: Claiming Head of AI, VP of AI, or Chief AI Officer titles without executive scope evidence.
Keyword stuffing with frontier AI terms like RLHF, custom LLM training without evidence.
Missing architecture and platform design terms in a 94.7% prevalence category.
Using research-heavy framing when research roles are only 10.5% of the market.
Inflating compliance keywords beyond documented GDPR/TCF or policy control experience.

what_good_looks_like: Architecture achievements quantified with systems launched, adoption metrics, latency, throughput, uptime.
Reliability proof includes incident rate reduction, MTTR, SLO attainment, error budget metrics.
Evaluation and guardrails achievements show quality score improvement, error reduction, acceptance rates.
Delivery acceleration metrics: cycle time, lead time, PR turnaround, automation coverage.
Integration outcomes: partner onboarding time, failure rate reduction, customer adoption.
Mentoring impact quantified: engineers coached, promotions influenced, design review improvements.
common_failures: Vague architecture claims without delivery or scale outcomes.
Reliability work described without operational metrics.
AI platform achievements missing evaluation or quality control metrics.
Mentoring described without measurable team impact.
Integration work lacking throughput or adoption numbers.
Using relative improvements without baselines or absolute figures.

what_to_adjust_per_jd: Scale metrics to company stage: startup adoption rates vs enterprise tenant counts.
Emphasize reliability metrics when JD mentions production readiness or operational excellence.
Foreground evaluation metrics when JD emphasizes AI quality or trustworthy outputs.
Highlight delivery speed metrics when JD mentions fast iteration or time-to-market.
company_domain_overrides: Consulting: emphasize client delivery timelines, integration success rates, stakeholder satisfaction.
Enterprise SaaS: focus on scalability metrics, multi-tenant performance, uptime guarantees.
Cybersecurity: prioritize guardrail effectiveness, policy violation rates, audit compliance.
Fintech: emphasize reliability, latency, and regulatory compliance metrics.
region_overrides: Remote-first (50%): include distributed team delivery metrics, cross-timezone collaboration outcomes.
EEA compliance: quantify GDPR or privacy compliance outcomes only when directly documented.

9_10: Every major achievement includes specific, verifiable metrics.
Architecture proof shows both delivery and scale outcomes with concrete numbers.
Reliability achievements include operational metrics: incident reduction, uptime, MTTR.
AI platform work quantifies evaluation improvements, guardrail effectiveness, adoption.
Mentoring impact is measurable: promotions, team velocity improvement, quality gains.
Metrics are appropriately scoped to evidenced responsibilities.
7_8: Most achievements include metrics but 1-2 key areas lack quantification.
Architecture and delivery metrics are strong but AI quality metrics are weaker.
Some metrics use relative improvements without clear baselines.
Mentoring or leadership impact is described but not fully quantified.
5_6: Mix of quantified and vague achievements.
Key category signals like architecture or reliability lack concrete metrics.
AI platform work is described functionally without quality or adoption outcomes.
Metrics present but not clearly tied to candidate's direct contribution.
0_4: Achievements lack meaningful quantification.
Generic descriptions of architecture or platform work without outcomes.
Metrics appear inflated or inconsistent with role scope.
No operational, delivery, or quality metrics despite category requirements.

red_flags: Metrics that exceed plausible scope for the role or company size.
Claiming team-wide or org-wide outcomes without evidence of ownership.
Inconsistent metrics across similar achievements.
Business outcome metrics (revenue, P&L) when role scope is senior IC or player-coach.
Publication or research metrics in a delivery-focused category.

what_good_looks_like: Architecture ownership is clearly demonstrated as the primary category signal at 94.7%.
Production AI platform work includes delivery, reliability, evaluation, and governance evidence.
Technical depth shows hands-on engineering credibility without overfitting to one language.
Cross-functional collaboration and communication are prominent as 81.6% and 68.4% signals.
Mentoring and technical leadership present without overstating people management.
Greenfield plus optimization experience shows breadth at 52.6% category prevalence.
common_failures: Missing architecture proof despite being a 94.7% category requirement.
Framing as pure research or model science in a production-delivery category.
Claiming executive org leadership when evidence supports player-coach scope.
Over-emphasizing one technology stack when the category is stack-agnostic.
Missing reliability or observability evidence despite 81% category weighting.
No evaluation or guardrails evidence despite being category differentiators.

what_to_adjust_per_jd: Weight architecture vs hands-on coding based on JD emphasis on design vs implementation.
Adjust AI method depth: RAG, agents, orchestration based on JD-specific requirements.
Match cloud and container stack to JD requirements when evidenced.
Emphasize enterprise integration when JD mentions partner APIs or system connectivity.
Surface domain expertise only when JD explicitly requires it and evidence supports it.
company_domain_overrides: Consulting (13.2%): foreground stakeholder translation, enterprise integration, partner delivery.
Enterprise SaaS (10.5%): emphasize multi-tenant architecture, scalability, integration patterns.
Cybersecurity (7.9%): highlight guardrails, policy controls, security architecture when evidenced.
Healthcare (5.3%): surface compliance and governance only with direct evidence.
region_overrides: Remote-first (50%): demonstrate distributed delivery credibility and async collaboration.
EEA: do not overstate regional compliance authority beyond documented experience.
Multi-country delivery: show cross-cultural communication when JD emphasizes global teams.

9_10: All JD must-haves are directly addressed with evidenced achievements.
Architecture ownership is unmistakably clear with production delivery proof.
AI platform work includes evaluation, reliability, and governance as appropriate.
Technical depth matches JD stack requirements without inflation.
Leadership scope accurately reflects player-coach or architect positioning.
Domain and region alignment is precise to JD requirements.
7_8: Most JD requirements addressed but 1-2 areas have weaker coverage.
Architecture proof is strong but AI quality controls are less prominent.
Stack alignment is good but misses some JD-specific technologies.
Leadership framing is appropriate but could be sharper.
5_6: Core JD requirements partially addressed.
Architecture evidence exists but is not prominently positioned.
Mix of aligned and misaligned signals across the CV.
Some scope inflation or misalignment in leadership framing.
0_4: Major JD requirements are unaddressed or poorly evidenced.
Missing architecture proof in a category where it is 94.7% prevalence.
Significant framing misalignment: research-heavy when delivery is required.
Executive claims without supporting scope evidence.
Stack or domain claims that contradict JD requirements.

red_flags: Claiming formal people management when JD indicates senior IC or player-coach scope.
Research or publication framing when JD emphasizes production delivery.
Missing architecture or platform design evidence for this category.
Domain expertise claims without supporting evidence.
Stack claims that contradict or exceed evidenced experience.

what_good_looks_like: Player-coach leadership: combines hands-on technical contribution with team influence.
Architectural authority: drives design decisions and technical standards across teams.
Stakeholder translation: communicates technical complexity to business and product partners.
Mentoring credibility: develops engineers through design review, coaching, and guidance.
Technical influence: shapes direction without requiring formal reporting lines.
Risk communication: educates stakeholders on technical debt, tradeoffs, and system health.
common_failures: Inflating to executive leadership when evidence supports architect or player-coach scope.
Claiming formal org management without documented reporting lines or hiring authority.
Missing stakeholder translation evidence despite 81.6% collaboration signal.
Understating technical leadership when evidence supports architect positioning.
Using generic leadership language without specific influence examples.

what_to_adjust_per_jd: Increase stakeholder emphasis when JD mentions cross-functional or enterprise alignment.
Foreground mentoring when JD explicitly mentions team development or coaching.
Emphasize architectural authority when JD focuses on design leadership or technical vision.
Include hiring process involvement only when JD mentions team building and evidence supports it.
company_domain_overrides: Consulting: emphasize client-facing influence and enterprise stakeholder management.
Enterprise SaaS: highlight cross-team collaboration and platform ownership.
Startups: show technical leadership breadth without overstating formal hierarchy.
region_overrides: Remote-first (50%): demonstrate distributed leadership and async decision-making.
EEA multi-country: show cross-cultural communication when teams span regions.

9_10: Clear player-coach positioning with both technical depth and team influence.
Stakeholder translation is concrete with specific examples and outcomes.
Mentoring impact is documented with promotions, skill development, or team growth.
Architectural authority is evident through design decisions, standards, and technical direction.
Leadership scope matches evidence without inflation to executive framing.
7_8: Player-coach elements present but one area (technical or leadership) is stronger.
Stakeholder translation mentioned but less concretely demonstrated.
Mentoring evident but impact is less precisely quantified.
Architectural authority is clear but influence scope could be sharper.
5_6: Leadership framing is generic without specific player-coach examples.
Stakeholder translation is implied but not clearly demonstrated.
Technical or mentoring credibility present but not both.
Some ambiguity about leadership scope versus formal management.
0_4: Leadership claims exceed evidenced scope.
Executive framing used when evidence supports IC or player-coach.
Missing stakeholder translation despite category requirements.
No mentoring or technical influence evidence.
Formal org management claims without documented reporting lines.

red_flags: Claiming Head of AI, VP, or Director titles without executive scope evidence.
Formal people management framing when evidence shows mentoring only.
Budget or P&L ownership claims when category shows 0% prevalence.
Org-building claims when hiring is only 5.3% and org_building is 10.5%.
Unsupported people-management framing that exceeds documented reporting lines.
Executive boardroom or C-suite language in a player-coach category.

what_good_looks_like: All metrics are traceable to documented achievements in the master CV.
Titles match actual positions held; no inflation to executive or research roles.
AI capabilities are bounded to evidenced work: integration, evaluation, guardrails.
Technical skills listed are supported by project or role evidence.
Leadership scope matches documented responsibilities without org-building inflation.
Domain expertise claims are supported by specific role or project evidence.
common_failures: Fabricating or inflating metrics beyond documented achievements.
Using executive titles when evidence supports senior IC or player-coach.
Claiming custom LLM training, fine-tuning, or RLHF without evidence.
Overstating compliance or governance authority beyond documented scope.
Adding PhD or publication credentials that do not exist.
Claiming formal reporting lines or team sizes without evidence.

what_to_adjust_per_jd: Verify all JD-aligned claims against master CV evidence before including.
Do not add domain expertise to match JD if not evidenced.
Do not inflate leadership scope to match JD executive requirements.
Verify stack claims against documented project and role evidence.
company_domain_overrides: Do not claim healthcare AI expertise without documented healthcare projects.
Do not claim fintech regulatory experience without evidenced compliance work.
Do not overstate cybersecurity credentials beyond documented guardrails work.
Do not add consulting experience or enterprise scale without evidence.
region_overrides: Do not claim broad EEA AI governance leadership without documented authority.
Do not add GDPR expertise beyond documented TCF or privacy compliance work.
Do not fabricate multi-country leadership scope.

9_10: Every claim traces directly to master CV evidence.
Titles match documented positions exactly.
Metrics are consistent with role scope and company context.
AI capabilities bounded to evidenced integration, evaluation, and guardrails work.
Leadership framing matches documented player-coach or architect scope.
No unsupported domain, compliance, or technical claims.
7_8: Nearly all claims are evidenced with minor ambiguities.
Titles are accurate but framing could imply slightly broader scope.
Metrics are credible but one or two lack clear provenance.
AI claims are appropriate but some terminology is slightly forward.
5_6: Most claims are evidenced but some require inference.
Title framing is accurate but leadership scope language is ambiguous.
Some metrics appear plausible but are not directly documented.
AI or technical claims include some unevidenced capabilities.
0_4: Multiple claims lack evidence support.
Title inflation to executive or research roles without evidence.
Metrics appear fabricated or significantly inflated.
AI claims include fine-tuning, RLHF, or custom training without evidence.
Leadership claims include formal org management without documented reports.
Domain expertise fabricated to match JD requirements.

red_flags: Unsourced metrics that cannot be traced to master CV achievements.
Fabricated titles: Head of AI, VP, Director, Chief AI Officer without evidence.
PhD or publication inflation in a category with 0% PhD requirement.
Unsupported scope of direct reports or team sizes.
Custom LLM training, fine-tuning, or RLHF claims without documentation.
Full MLOps ownership claims beyond evidenced responsibilities.
Vector database architecture claims exceeding actual role requirements.
Mechanistic interpretability or research claims in a delivery-focused category.
Budget or P&L ownership claims when baseline shows 0% prevalence.
Broad EEA AI governance claims beyond documented GDPR/TCF work.
Enterprise AI transformation leader framing without architecture or delivery proof.
Agentic AI expert claims without shipped orchestration, evaluation, or governance evidence.

pass_criteria: Architecture ownership is clearly evidenced as the primary category signal.
Production AI platform or integration evidence is present.
Delivery outcomes are quantified with concrete metrics.
Cross-functional collaboration or stakeholder translation is demonstrated.
Technical depth is shown through hands-on engineering evidence.
fail_conditions: No architecture or platform design evidence despite 94.7% category prevalence.
Missing delivery or production outcome metrics.
No technical depth evidence; purely management or strategy framing.
Zero stakeholder translation or collaboration signals.
Research-only framing without production delivery proof.

pass_criteria: All title claims match documented positions.
All metrics trace to evidenced achievements.
AI capabilities are bounded to documented work.
Leadership scope matches evidenced player-coach or architect positioning.
Domain and compliance claims are supported by specific evidence.
fail_conditions: Title inflation to Head of AI, VP, or Director without evidence.
Metrics that exceed plausible scope for role or company.
Custom LLM training, fine-tuning, or RLHF claims without documentation.
Formal org management claims without documented reporting lines.
Budget or P&L ownership claims in a 0% prevalence category.
Broad EEA governance authority beyond documented compliance work.
PhD or publication claims without credentials.
Enterprise AI transformation claims without architecture or delivery proof.

pass_criteria: Technical depth and leadership influence are balanced appropriately.
Production delivery emphasis over research or experimental framing.
Stakeholder translation present without executive business ownership.
Mentoring credibility without formal people management inflation.
fail_conditions: Executive or C-suite positioning in a senior IC / player-coach category.
Research scientist or model owner framing in a delivery-focused market.
Pure people management framing without technical architecture evidence.
Business owner or P&L framing when evidence supports technical leadership.
Strategy-only positioning without hands-on engineering credibility.

scoring_guidance.how_to_interpret_executive_presence: For this architect-first player-coach category, executive_presence measures player-coach AI platform leadership: architectural authority, stakeholder translation, mentoring credibility, and technical influence. Do NOT interpret as literal executive leadership, C-suite presence, boardroom communication, or P&L ownership. Penalize claims of formal org management, budget ownership, or executive titles when evidence supports senior IC or player-coach scope.
scoring_guidance.how_to_penalize_unsupported_claims: Deduct proportionally based on claim severity: fabricated metrics or titles warrant 2-3 point deductions in anti_hallucination; unsupported AI capabilities like custom training or RLHF warrant 1-2 points; scope inflation in leadership or domain expertise warrants 1-2 points. Multiple unsupported claims should compound. Any single egregious fabrication (false credentials, invented metrics, completely fabricated roles) fails the unsafe_claim_gate regardless of other scores.
scoring_guidance.how_to_handle_category_vs_job_tradeoffs: Category signals establish the baseline: architecture 94.7%, delivery 95.2%, reliability 81%, evaluation 52.4%. Job-specific requirements can shift emphasis within these ranges but should not override category fundamentals. If a JD requests executive leadership but the candidate evidence supports player-coach, maintain accurate framing rather than inflating. Stack-specific requirements can be weighted when evidenced but should not dominate over category table-stakes.

evidence_ledger.rubric_rule: Architecture ownership is the primary category signal at 94.7% prevalence and 100% valued evidence in deep analyses.
Senior IC scope dominates at 86.8% while manager is 5.3% and director is 5.3%, requiring player-coach not executive framing.
Delivery is the second-strongest valued evidence at 95.2% of deep analyses, ahead of most AI-specific methods.
Reliability at 81% and evaluation at 52.4% are category differentiators that distinguish production AI from research framing.
Hiring at 5.3%, org_building at 10.5%, and budget_pnl at 0% establish that formal executive management claims are unsafe.
Research-heavy roles at 10.5% and PhD requirement at 0% mean research or publication framing misaligns with category.
Custom LLM training, fine-tuning, and RLHF are explicitly marked unsafe in the baseline do-not-claim ledger.
GDPR and data privacy each appear only 5.3%, so broad EEA governance claims exceed safe evidence scope.
Collaboration at 81.6% and communication at 68.4% are the strongest soft-skill signals, supporting stakeholder translation emphasis.
No single technical keyword exceeds 36.8% (Python), so ATS optimization should not overfit to one stack.
Remote-first positions at 50% mean distributed delivery credibility is a material category signal.
Baseline combined fit score of 7.84 establishes GOOD tier with architecture and AI platform as strongest supported signals.
